# Language and Identity: How Multilingualism Shapes Cultures

![Oluwaseun](https://miro.medium.com/v2/da:true/resize:fill:64:64/0*n2JZcxI-lbTdWI8I)

Language is not merely a tool for communication; it is a powerful force that intricately weaves into the fabric of our identity and shapes the cultural landscapes we inhabit. In this exploration, we embark on a journey to unveil the profound role that language plays in the intricate process of identity formation. Beyond mere words and phrases, language becomes a mirror reflecting the values, beliefs, and nuances of the cultures it embodies.

# Unveiling the Role of Language in Shaping Identity

At its core, language is a vehicle through which individuals express their thoughts, emotions, and experiences. However, it transcends the functional aspects of communication; it becomes a vessel for the transmission of cultural heritage, social norms, and collective memory. The way we speak, the words we choose, and the languages we embrace are pivotal elements in constructing and expressing our individual and collective identities. Language, in essence, is a dynamic mirror reflecting who we are, where we come from, and how we perceive the world around us.

As we delve into the intricate layers of language’s role in identity formation, we will explore how it serves as a cultural compass, guiding individuals through the diverse landscapes of their heritage. From the nuances embedded in dialects to the eloquence of poetry, language becomes the artist that paints the vivid strokes of identity on the canvas of our lives.

# Exploring the Interplay Between Multilingualism and Cultural Diversity

In our interconnected world, the phenomenon of multilingualism introduces an additional layer of complexity and richness to the cultural tapestry. Multilingualism is not merely the ability to speak multiple languages; it is a dynamic interplay between diverse linguistic systems that reflects the multifaceted nature of the communities we inhabit. This interplay is a symphony of voices, each contributing to the harmonious diversity that defines our cultural identities.

Through the exploration of multilingualism, we will navigate the ways in which individuals move seamlessly between languages, adapting their expressions to the cultural context at hand. The interplay between languages creates a unique fusion, fostering an environment where cultural diversity is not only embraced but celebrated. It is in this intermingling of languages that we discover the ability of multilingualism to bridge cultural gaps, fostering understanding and unity across linguistic divides.

As we embark on this exploration, we invite you to contemplate the intricate dance between language and identity, recognizing the profound impact of multilingualism on the cultural richness that defines our shared human experience. Join us in unraveling the threads that connect language, identity, and cultural diversity, and discover the depth of the interplay that shapes the very essence of who we are.

# The Power of Language in Identity Formation

Language serves as a dynamic force, intricately entwined with the process of identity formation. Beyond its utilitarian role in communication, language possesses the power to shape, define, and express our individual and collective identities. In this section, we delve into the profound impact of language on the intricate journey of identity formation, exploring how it functions as more than just a medium of expression.

# Language as a Mirror of Cultural Values

At the heart of identity lies culture, and language stands as a faithful mirror reflecting the values, norms, and intricacies of the cultures we inherit. The words we choose, the idioms we employ, and the linguistic nuances embedded in our speech patterns serve as cultural imprints, revealing the rich tapestry of our heritage.

When individuals communicate in a particular language, they carry with them the accumulated wisdom, traditions, and shared experiences of their cultural background. Language becomes a vessel through which cultural values are transmitted across generations, fostering a sense of continuity and connection to one’s roots. Whether it’s the formality of address, the choice of pronouns, or the expressions of politeness, these linguistic elements encapsulate cultural values that contribute to the multifaceted nature of identity.

As individuals engage in linguistic expression, they unconsciously draw from the reservoir of cultural meanings embedded in their language. The choice of metaphors, the rhythm of speech, and even the structure of storytelling are all manifestations of cultural values interwoven into the very fabric of language. Thus, language emerges not only as a means of communication but as a living repository of cultural identity, allowing individuals to express, celebrate, and perpetuate their cultural heritage.

![]()

# Linguistic Determinism: How Language Influences Thought and Perception

In the intricate dance between language and identity, the concept of linguistic determinism comes to the forefront. Linguistic determinism posits that language not only reflects but also shapes thought and perception. The language we use influences the way we conceptualize the world, construct meaning, and interpret our experiences.

The vocabulary available in a language can shape the cognitive categories individuals use to organize their thoughts. For example, languages with multiple words for specific shades of color can lead speakers of those languages to perceive and distinguish those colors more readily than speakers of languages with fewer specific color terms.

Moreover, the grammatical structures inherent in a language can influence the way individuals perceive causality, relationships, and even time. For instance, languages that grammatically distinguish between the present and future may lead speakers to conceptualize the future as more distant and abstract compared to speakers of languages without such distinctions.

In essence, linguistic determinism highlights the intricate relationship between language, thought processes, and worldview. As individuals navigate the linguistic landscape, they engage in a continuous dance between expressing their identity through language and, in turn, having their identity shaped by the very language they use.

In the subsequent sections, we will further explore how multilingualism amplifies these dynamics, creating a rich and nuanced interplay that contributes to the mosaic of identity within diverse cultural contexts.

# Multilingualism Across Cultures

Multilingualism stands as a testament to the diversity of human communication, reflecting the intricate ways in which languages coexist and intermingle within societies worldwide. In this section, we embark on a journey to explore the manifold expressions of multilingualism, recognizing its prevalence as a vibrant and dynamic aspect of cultural identity across the globe.

# Diverse Multilingual Practices Worldwide

Across continents, countries, and communities, multilingualism manifests in an array of practices, each reflecting the unique socio-cultural context in which it flourishes. From regions with longstanding linguistic diversity to those shaped by historical migrations and globalization, the coexistence of multiple languages is a hallmark of the human experience.

In some communities, multilingualism is woven into the fabric of daily life, where individuals effortlessly switch between languages depending on the context, audience, or communicative goal. This fluidity in language use is not a sign of confusion but a rich demonstration of linguistic versatility and cultural adaptation. Whether it’s code-switching within a sentence or navigating a multilingual marketplace, these practices embody the dynamic nature of multilingual societies.

In regions with a history of cultural convergence, such as trade routes, crossroads of civilizations, or colonial legacies, multilingualism often emerges as a natural consequence of diverse cultural interactions. Here, languages may coexist harmoniously, each contributing to the cultural mosaic while maintaining its distinct identity. This cohabitation of languages fosters an environment where individuals seamlessly draw from linguistic repertoires, celebrating the diversity that characterizes their cultural identity.

Moreover, in some indigenous communities, multilingualism is deeply embedded in the fabric of cultural traditions. Native languages, often intertwined with sacred practices, oral histories, and traditional knowledge, play a crucial role in preserving the unique identity of these communities. In these contexts, multilingualism becomes a vehicle for transmitting ancestral wisdom and strengthening cultural bonds across generations.

# Case Studies: Examining Multilingualism’s Impact on Cultural Identity

To delve deeper into the intricate relationship between multilingualism and cultural identity, we turn our attention to case studies that illuminate the diverse ways in which multiple languages shape and enrich the cultural tapestry.

# *Case Study 1: Switzerland*

In Switzerland, a country with four national languages — German, French, Italian, and Romansh — the coexistence of these languages reflects the nation’s unique cultural and historical makeup. Each linguistic region not only speaks its designated language but also contributes to the broader Swiss identity. Multilingualism in Switzerland is a source of national pride, fostering a sense of unity amid linguistic diversity.

# *Case Study 2: India*

India, with its rich linguistic diversity, provides a fascinating case study of multilingualism. The country is home to numerous languages, each with its own script, dialects, and cultural nuances. The linguistic landscape of India reflects the intricate interplay of historical, geographical, and cultural factors. Language becomes a key marker of regional identity, shaping social interactions, literature, and cultural expressions.

# *Case Study 3: Canada*

In Canada, where English and French are official languages, the recognition of linguistic diversity is enshrined in the country’s identity. The bilingual nature of Canada contributes to a mosaic of cultural expressions, where both English and French serve as vehicles for artistic, political, and everyday communication. This bilingualism not only reflects the nation’s commitment to inclusivity but also enhances the cultural richness of its society.

Through these case studies and others across the globe, we witness the dynamic interplay between multilingualism and cultural identity. Whether in the mountainous landscapes of Switzerland, the diverse tapestry of India, or the vast expanse of Canada, the coexistence of multiple languages shapes unique cultural identities, fostering a sense of belonging and contributing to the richness of human expression.

In the subsequent sections, we will delve into the ways in which multilingualism influences acculturation, education, and the preservation of cultural heritage within diverse societies.

# Language, Acculturation, and Cultural Adaptation

Having explored the diverse manifestations of multilingualism across cultures, we now delve into how the interplay between languages influences the processes of acculturation and cultural adaptation. In this section, we examine the pivotal role that language plays in shaping individuals’ experiences as they navigate and integrate into different cultural contexts.

# The Role of Language in Acculturation Processes

Acculturation refers to the cultural changes and psychological adjustments that occur when individuals from one cultural group come into contact with another. Language, as a primary medium of cultural expression, becomes a central factor in the acculturation journey. The proficiency in the language of the host culture significantly impacts an individual’s ability to engage, connect, and adapt to the new cultural environment.

Language proficiency serves as a bridge, facilitating communication and fostering a sense of belonging. Individuals who acquire proficiency in the language of the host culture often experience a smoother acculturation process. The ability to communicate effectively opens doors to social interactions, educational opportunities, and professional integration, contributing to a more seamless adaptation to the new cultural milieu.

Conversely, challenges in language acquisition can pose barriers to acculturation. Limited language proficiency may lead to feelings of isolation, difficulties in forming social connections, and obstacles in accessing essential services. As individuals navigate the complexities of acculturation, language serves as both a facilitator and a potential hurdle, influencing the degree to which individuals can actively participate in and contribute to their adopted cultural context.

# How Multilingualism Facilitates Cultural Adaptation and Integration

In multilingual societies, individuals often possess the ability to navigate between multiple languages, a skill that becomes particularly advantageous during acculturation. Multilingual individuals can seamlessly adapt their linguistic expressions to various social contexts, enabling them to engage with both their heritage culture and the new host culture.

Multilingualism enhances cultural adaptability by providing individuals with the flexibility to choose the language that best suits the communicative demands of a given situation. For example, in a multicultural workplace, a multilingual individual may effortlessly switch between languages, fostering effective communication with colleagues from diverse linguistic backgrounds. This linguistic versatility contributes to a more inclusive and harmonious cultural integration.

Furthermore, in multicultural communities, the presence of multiple languages often signifies an openness to cultural diversity. Multilingual environments encourage the celebration of various cultural expressions and create spaces where individuals can maintain connections to their heritage while actively participating in the host culture. This duality fosters a sense of bicultural identity, where individuals embrace and navigate multiple cultural frameworks.

In the subsequent sections, we will explore how language preservation and educational approaches contribute to the intricate dynamics of multilingualism, playing crucial roles in fostering cultural diversity and identity within diverse societies.

# Bilingualism in Educational Systems

As we continue our exploration of the interplay between language and cultural identity, this section delves into the realm of education. Bilingualism, the use of two languages in educational settings, plays a crucial role in shaping the cultural and linguistic landscape of societies. In this section, we examine the impact of bilingual education on cultural diversity and identity.

![]()

# Multilingual Education: Nurturing Cultural and Linguistic Diversity

In diverse societies, educational systems face the challenge of accommodating students from various linguistic and cultural backgrounds. Multilingual education emerges as a response to this challenge, aiming to create inclusive learning environments that celebrate and preserve linguistic diversity.

One approach to multilingual education involves incorporating students’ native languages into the curriculum alongside the dominant language of instruction. This not only facilitates academic learning but also fosters a sense of cultural pride and identity. Students see their languages and cultures acknowledged and valued within the educational framework, contributing to a more inclusive and culturally sensitive learning experience.

Multilingual education recognizes that language is not only a tool for communication but also a vehicle for transmitting cultural knowledge and heritage. By incorporating multiple languages into the educational landscape, students gain a broader understanding of their cultural identity and heritage, fostering a sense of belonging within the school community.

# Challenges and Benefits of Bilingual Education

While bilingual education offers numerous benefits, it is not without its challenges. Adequately implementing and sustaining bilingual programs requires addressing linguistic, pedagogical, and cultural considerations. Ensuring that students have access to high-quality education in both languages, recruiting bilingual teachers, and creating culturally relevant learning materials are crucial aspects of successful bilingual education.

Bilingual education also faces challenges related to societal attitudes toward language and cultural diversity. In some contexts, there may be a perception that proficiency in a dominant language is more valuable than maintaining proficiency in heritage languages. Overcoming these attitudes involves advocating for the recognition of the inherent value of linguistic diversity and promoting the benefits of bilingualism in academic, cognitive, and cultural domains.

In addition to academic advantages, bilingual education equips students with valuable skills such as cross-cultural communication, adaptability, and a broader perspective on global issues. As students navigate their educational journey in a multilingual environment, they not only acquire knowledge but also develop a nuanced understanding of the interconnectedness of language, culture, and identity.

# Language Preservation and Cultural Heritage

In this section, we explore the vital connection between language preservation and the safeguarding of cultural heritage. Languages serve as carriers of unique cultural knowledge, traditions, and narratives, making their preservation essential for maintaining the rich tapestry of human diversity.

# The Vital Link Between Language Preservation and Cultural Identity

Languages are not mere communication tools; they encapsulate the collective wisdom, history, and cultural expressions of communities. As languages fade away, so too does a significant part of the cultural heritage they carry. Language preservation thus becomes a crucial endeavor in safeguarding cultural identities and ensuring the continuity of cultural traditions across generations.

Indigenous languages, in particular, play a pivotal role in preserving the unique identities of indigenous communities. These languages are often deeply intertwined with traditional practices, ceremonies, and oral histories that convey the spiritual and cultural foundations of indigenous societies. The erosion of these languages poses a direct threat to the preservation of indigenous cultures.

Efforts to preserve endangered languages involve documenting linguistic resources, creating language learning materials, and implementing educational programs that prioritize the use of endangered languages in formal and informal settings. By revitalizing and maintaining these languages, communities can actively participate in shaping their cultural narratives and reinforcing a strong sense of cultural identity.

Language and Social Dynamics

As we delve deeper into the multifaceted relationship between language and culture, this section focuses on the role of language in shaping social dynamics within multilingual communities. Language is not only a means of communication but also a dynamic force that influences social interactions, relationships, and the formation of community identity.

# Linguistic Landscapes: How Language Shapes Social Environments

In multilingual societies, the linguistic landscape is a vibrant tapestry that reflects the diversity of languages spoken within a community. Signs, advertisements, and public spaces showcase the coexistence of multiple languages, creating a visual representation of the community’s linguistic richness. Linguistic landscapes contribute to a sense of belonging, allowing individuals to see their languages and cultures acknowledged in the public sphere.

The visibility of multiple languages in public spaces not only fosters inclusivity but also acts as a reminder of the interconnectedness of language and identity. It reinforces the idea that linguistic diversity is not a barrier but a source of strength, contributing to the creation of socially harmonious environments where individuals feel valued for their linguistic and cultural contributions.

# Multilingualism’s Impact on Social Interaction and Community Dynamics

In everyday social interactions, language serves as a powerful tool for building connections and expressing cultural nuances. Multilingual individuals navigate a complex web of linguistic choices, deciding which language to use based on the context, the relationships involved, and the cultural dynamics at play. This linguistic flexibility enables individuals to form connections with others who share a common language while also accommodating those from different linguistic backgrounds.

In multicultural communities, the ability to engage in multilingual conversations fosters a sense of unity and mutual understanding. It creates a space where individuals can appreciate the diversity of languages spoken within the community, leading to the emergence of a shared identity that transcends linguistic differences. Multilingualism becomes a bridge that connects people, encouraging the exchange of ideas, traditions, and cultural practices.

Moreover, multilingual social dynamics contribute to the development of individuals with a heightened awareness of cultural sensitivity. As individuals navigate the intricacies of multiple languages, they become adept at recognizing and respecting diverse communication styles, fostering an environment of mutual respect and inclusivity within the community.

# Language and Globalization

In an era characterized by increased connectivity and global interdependence, the role of language in shaping cultural identity extends beyond local communities. This section explores how language functions in the globalized world, with a particular focus on English as a global lingua franca and the implications of this linguistic phenomenon on cultural identity.

# The Role of Language in the Globalized World

As the world becomes more interconnected through technology, trade, and cultural exchange, language plays a pivotal role in facilitating communication across borders. In this globalized landscape, English has emerged as a dominant global lingua franca — a common language used for communication between speakers of different native languages.

The widespread use of English in global communication is evident in international business, diplomacy, academia, and popular culture. English serves as a medium through which individuals from diverse linguistic backgrounds can communicate and collaborate, transcending linguistic barriers. However, the prevalence of English as a global language also raises important questions about linguistic diversity, cultural representation, and the potential impact on local languages.

# English as a Global Lingua Franca: Implications for Cultural Identity

While English as a global lingua franca facilitates communication on a global scale, it also raises concerns about the potential homogenization of culture. The dominance of English in various domains, including media, entertainment, and digital communication, can influence cultural perceptions and expressions.

In the globalized world, individuals and communities must navigate the tension between embracing the opportunities afforded by a common global language and preserving the linguistic and cultural diversity that defines their identity. For many, maintaining proficiency in their native languages becomes a way of preserving cultural heritage and resisting the potential erosion of local languages in the face of global linguistic trends.

Moreover, the influence of English in the globalized world introduces questions of linguistic privilege and power dynamics. Proficiency in English is often seen as an asset in accessing economic opportunities, higher education, and participation in global discourse. This reality creates challenges for individuals and communities whose native languages may be marginalized in the global arena.

# Code-Switching and Cultural Fluidity

In multilingual environments, individuals often engage in a linguistic phenomenon known as code-switching. This section explores code-switching as a cultural practice, shedding light on how individuals navigate the complexities of multilingualism by seamlessly shifting between languages in their daily interactions.

# Exploring Code-Switching as a Cultural Phenomenon

Code-switching refers to the practice of alternating between two or more languages or language varieties within a single conversation or discourse. Rather than a sign of linguistic confusion, code-switching is a dynamic and intentional act that individuals use to navigate social and cultural contexts effectively.

In multilingual communities, code-switching is a common and natural occurrence. Individuals may code-switch based on factors such as the formality of a situation, the participants involved, or the topic of conversation. This linguistic flexibility enables individuals to express themselves more precisely and connect with others on a deeper cultural level.

# Navigating Cultural Fluidity Through Multilingual Practices

Code-switching is not only a linguistic phenomenon but also a cultural practice that reflects the fluidity of identity in multilingual contexts. When individuals code-switch, they are not merely switching languages; they are navigating a complex interplay of cultural nuances, social dynamics, and situational appropriateness.

# Multilingual individuals may code-switch to:

Express Cultural Nuances: Certain concepts or emotions may be more accurately expressed in one language over another. Code-switching allows individuals to convey cultural nuances that may not have a direct equivalent in another language.

Understanding code-switching as a cultural practice highlights the richness of multilingual environments. Rather than viewing it as a deviation from linguistic norms, recognizing code-switching as a cultural phenomenon emphasizes the adaptability and resourcefulness of individuals in navigating the complexities of identity within diverse linguistic landscapes.

SUMMARY:
In a globalized world, business communications are increasingly multilingual and linguistic diversity is a defining characteristic of virtually all MNEs, particularly those operating in countries with multiple languages (Karhunen et al., 2018) While employees may be required to speak one corporate language within their organization, there is evidence that one's native language is a significant element of identity and can influence workplace relationships and interactions with colleagues (Hinds, Neeley & Crampton, 2014; Peltokorpi & Pudelko, 2021). First, our study adds nuance to the fast-growing literature on language diversity in IB research, particularly more nascent work on intra-country language diversity, by showing how language intersects with other salient identity attributes (e.g., gender, education) to jointly shape the development of language-based clusters within MNEs. Second, we develop an empirically grounded process model to depict how these attributes change the composition of language clusters over time, thereby extending prior work, which has conceptualized language clusters primarily in static terms.

Abstract
--------

Research on language diversity in international business has shown that language is a core element of one's social identity and employees in [MNEs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/company "Learn more about MNEs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") often form homophily-based clusters with those who share their native language. Such clusters can significantly influence communication and knowledge sharing processes within MNEs. However, research assumes that language-based clusters are monolithic in nature. In this study of two MNE headquarters in [India](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/india "Learn more about India from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), we develop a model to depict the dynamic processes by which language intersects with other salient identity attributes such as gender, regional dialects, and education to change the composition of language-based clusters and alter group dynamics and organizational processes.

1. Introduction
---------------

Over the past few decades, increased globalization has fueled the debate in international business (IB) regarding the consideration and management of diversity ([Fitzsimmons, Özbilgin, Thomas & Nkomo, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0023); [Jonsen, Maznevski & Schneider, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0034)). The research on diversity in IB has mainly focused on national-level accounts of cultural values ([Tenzer, Terjesen & Harzing, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0083)) as the primary source of diversity in multinational [enterprises](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/company "Learn more about enterprises from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (MNEs), which do not capture individual-level variance or the full complexity of social processes and outcomes in intercultural settings ([Peltokorpi & Pudelko, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0061)). Moreover “the field of diversity is not so diverse itself” as much of the IB literature is dominated by US and European-centric approaches ([Jonsen et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0034), p.52). Scholars have increasingly argued that the tendency of IB researchers to focus on national level differences fails to account for heterogeneity within nations ([Tung, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0085)), meaning that potentially important differences between various societal subgroups or geographic regions are not explored in detail, or are ignored altogether. A “fallacious assumption” ([Tung, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0085), p.42) of homogeneity within nations, and the tendency for many IB scholars to equate cross-cultural with cross-national, has led to a lacuna in our understanding of important within country differences, which could have significant implications for [IB and management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/international-business-and-management "Learn more about IB and management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Because IB research has mainly focused on national culture as the primary source of diversity in international organizations, the roles of gender, race and language are often overlooked (e.g., [Ciuk, Śliwa & Harzing, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0018); [Fitzsimmons et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0024); [Jonson et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0034); [Summerville et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0077)). Our study's focus on language and its relationship with other aspects of identity, such as regional dialects and gender, represents a more holistic approach. In a globalized world, business communications are increasingly multilingual and linguistic diversity is a defining characteristic of virtually all MNEs, particularly those operating in countries with multiple languages ([Karhunen et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0036)) While employees may be required to speak one corporate language within their organization, there is evidence that one's native language is a significant element of identity and can influence workplace relationships and interactions with colleagues ([Hinds, Neeley & Crampton, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0032); [Peltokorpi & Pudelko, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0061)). Prior research shows that individuals tend to form language groups or clusters with those who speak the same native language because of the innate desire to belong and feel valued ([Mäkelä, Kalla & Piekkari, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0051); [Fredriksson, Barner-Rasmussen & Piekkari, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0025)). These clusters represent informal structures based on individuals’ language proficiencies and provide informal sources of power and influence that can shape, or even threaten, formal lines of communication ([Marschan-Piekkari, Welch & Welch, 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0052)). They can also significantly influence patterns of communication and information exchange within MNEs as employees might share relevant information primarily with those in their language cluster, thereby creating faultlines and inhibiting the flow of information to other clusters (e.g., [Hinds et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0032)). This can create status imbalances within clusters and organizations wherein a few individuals become gatekeepers of information.

Most of the extant literature on language diversity and language-based clustering within MNEs highlights language-induced barriers or divisions, and their negative impacts on various facets of organizational life (e.g., [Hinds et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0032); [Peltokorpi & Pudelko, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0061)). Given the focus on language-based divisions, there is a tendency for this literature to implicitly adopt a static and one-dimensional view of language clusters. However, some scholars have suggested that language-based clusters may overlap with other identity-based groupings within organizations and, in this sense, their boundaries can be fuzzy and mutable (e.g., [Tenzer et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0083)). They have also astutely noted that such clustering can have both positive and negative consequences, implying that a more nuanced approach is required for theory development in this domain. Our study adopts such an approach and answers broader calls for examining the full complexity of social processes and outcomes in MNEs, by empirically examining the phenomenon of language clustering within two Indian MNEs. Theoretically, we integrate concepts from [social identity theory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/social-identity-theory "Learn more about social identity theory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [sociolinguistics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/sociolinguistics "Learn more about sociolinguistics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the [EDI](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/electronic-data-interchange "Learn more about EDI from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") literature, to provide a deeper understanding of how and why such clusters develop, and their implications for understanding diversity and group dynamics in international organizations. More specifically, we explore the following research questions: _What identity attributes influence an individual's association with language clusters within MNEs? How does the intersection of language and these identity attributes influence group dynamics within these clusters and their evolution? What are some positive and negative consequences of clusters for organizations, groups, and individuals?_

Our study contributes to the IB literature by providing novel theoretical and practical insights on how individuals manage linguistic diversity within multicultural and multilingual organizations and societies, a vitally important topic that constitutes a growing, yet still underdeveloped, area of IB research ([Brannen, Piekkari & Tietze, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0010); [Tenzer et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0083)). We make at least four distinctive contributions to the literature. First, our study adds nuance to the fast-growing literature on language diversity in IB research, particularly more nascent work on intra-country language diversity, by showing how language intersects with other salient identity attributes (e.g., gender, education) to jointly shape the development of language-based clusters within MNEs. Second, we develop an empirically grounded process model to depict how these attributes change the composition of language clusters over time, thereby extending prior work, which has conceptualized language clusters primarily in static terms. We hence provide a more dynamic account of why and how language-clusters emerge within MNEs, who tends to engage with (or avoid) them, and how and why they may divide and splinter over time. Third, our study is distinctive in that it offers a more balanced view of the impacts of language-based clusters in organizations, which the literature has framed mainly in a negative light. Importantly, our work suggests that language-clusters are sources of emotional and professional support for individuals in MNEs, which may actually facilitate communication in certain contexts. Fourth, the current research on language in IB is dominated by North America and Western Europe ([Karhunen et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0036); [Tenzer et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0083)). Although few scholars have explored contexts such as [Japan](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/japan "Learn more about Japan from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g., [Hinds et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0032); [Peltokorpi & Yamao, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0063)) China (e.g., [Lauring & Klitmøller, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0044); [Selmer, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0071)), [India](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/india "Learn more about India from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g., [Boussebaa, Sinha & Gabriel, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0009); [Kalra & Szymanski, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0035); [Kulkarni, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0040)) and Russia (e.g., [Latukha et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0042)), there is a scarcity of research on emerging countries. Our study extends the geographical scope of this research by highlighting the implications of language diversity in India, an understudied context, by focusing on the relationships between different identity attributes within two Indian MNEs.

Our paper proceeds as follows: Following a theory elaboration approach ([Bluhm et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0007); [Pratt, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0065)), we first ground our work in the literatures on [social identity theory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/social-identity-theory "Learn more about social identity theory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and language in IB and highlight the key research gaps that we aim to bridge. We then describe the research context and the comparative [case study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case study from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") methodology we used to gather and analyze our data, and we present key findings. We conclude with a discussion of our findings and their implications for theory, practice, and future research.

2. Theoretical framing
----------------------

### 2.1. An overview of social identity theory

Social identity theory, a theory of [social psychology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/social-psychology "Learn more about social psychology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that explains group processes and intergroup behaviors, posits that individuals derive their identities from group memberships, and they use these affiliations to distinguish between ingroup and outgroup members ([Tajfel, 1974](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0078), [1981](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0079)). It originated with Tajfel's work on social and cognitive aspects related to the perceptions of prejudice and discrimination and theorizes that a person's social identity is a function of their association with a particular group identity that is salient at a given time and their understanding of the relationship of their ingroup to salient outgroups. Individuals can derive their identities from various sources such as organization, gender, language, and ethnicity ([Ashforth & Mael, 1989](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0006); [Giles & Johnson, 1987](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0027)) and use different cognitive schemas to define such classifications. A central motive of such [identity construction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/identity-construction "Learn more about identity construction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the need to experience certainty and to feel connected to and accepted by certain others within an organization or a social context. A person's identity and categorization into social groups forms a basis for distinguishing between ingroup and outgroup members, while favoring the ingroup to maintain the underlying need for self-enhancement ([Turner & Tajfel, 1986](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0086)).

Social identity theory posits that it is common for individuals to retain multiple social identities such as gender, race, and minority status at any given point of time ([Abrams et al., 1990](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0002); [Ramarajan, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0067)), and that the impact of group identification depends on the salience of a particular social identity ([van Knippenberg, 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0091), p.359). Research also shows that individuals’ behaviors may change as the situational salience of those identities shifts ([Ramarajan, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0067)). For instance, for females, gender is often most salient when they are members of mixed-gender groups rather than female-only groups ([Abrams et al., 1990](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0002); [Cameron, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0015)). In female-only groups, other aspects such as ethnicity or language could be more salient.

Although [social identity theory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/social-identity-theory "Learn more about social identity theory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is well established in social psychology, its application in the context of linguistic diversity research is rather limited (see [Reiche et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0069), [Peltokorpi & Pudelko, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0061), and [Vaara et al., 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0088), [2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0089), for notable exceptions). This is surprising considering that the IB literature has long regarded language as an important marker of national identity and that individuals often attach unique emotional significance to their native language ([Giles & Johnson, 1987](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0027); [Mäkelä et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0051)). Accordingly, understanding the ways in which language shapes social identity is of central importance to IB scholars, and [social identity theory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/social-identity-theory "Learn more about social identity theory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") would seem an apt theoretical lens ([Peltokorpi & Pudelko, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0061)). However, the socio-psychological understanding of social identity theory has been critiqued for its simplistic conceptions of identity formation, and its unitary or linear views of demographic attributes, including language ([Lauring, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0043)). Some scholars have thus argued that this “essentialization” of demographic differences as a means of understanding identity construction is inadequate considering that identity formation in multinational settings is complex, context-dependent and fluid ([Vaara et al., 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0088)). Consequently, there is an opportunity to enrich social identity theory and IB scholarship alike by moving beyond static or deterministic conceptions of social identity and linguistic clusters toward a methodological and theoretical approach that offers more nuanced insights into the complex micro-dynamics of intra- and inter-group interactions and their relation to identity making in [MNEs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/company "Learn more about MNEs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This is a key aim of our study.

### 2.2. Linguistic identities and clustering

Consistent with the tenets of social identity theory, language use can influence individuals’ cognitive schemas, leading to development of harmful stereotypes and outgroup bias towards speakers of other languages, and ingroup favoritism towards speakers of one's own language ([Tajfel, 1981](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0079); [Turner & Tajfel, 1986](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0086)). Language can be defined as “a system for communicating thoughts and feelings that is understood by a particular community" and constitutes a social process through which human beings identify similar others and establish and maintain relationships with them ([Church-Morel & Bartel-Radic, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0017), p.13). Within the IB literature, these dynamics have been explained via the theoretical lens of interpersonal [homophily](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/homophily "Learn more about homophily from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (similarity) in the context of linguistic clustering within MNEs, where such clusters are construed as informal groupings of people who interact more frequently because of their shared language ([Mäkelä et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0051)). While these structures are informal, they have been theorized to have important implications for communications, knowledge sharing, and power dynamics within MNEs ([Kulkarni, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0040); [Marschan-Piekkari et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0052); [Peltokorpi & Pudelko, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0061)). Prior IB research has mainly focused on the role of corporate language policies and noted that enforcement of such policies can create power imbalances within MNEs ([Gaibrois & Nentwich, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0026); [Vaara et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0089)). On one hand, implementation of a corporate language can have positive career outcomes for proficient speakers ([Bordia & Bordia, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0008)), on the other, it can threaten linguistic identities of non-native/less fluent speakers, unless senior management can provide support to reduce the feelings of exclusion experienced by these individuals ([Ritz & Alfes, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0070)). Generally, resultant language barriers experienced by non-native speakers aggravate communication problems, thwart knowledge sharing, hamper interpersonal and group collaboration (e.g., [Harzing & Feely, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0030)), and hinder career progression for non-proficient speakers (e.g., [Latukha et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0042); [Peltokorpi & Vaara, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0062)). Such barriers hence become a source of stress, anxiety, frustration, and [shame](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/shame "Learn more about shame from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for non-native speakers ([Hinds et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0032); [Tenzer & Pudelko, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0082); [Neeley et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0056)) and exacerbate mistrust between native and non-native speakers, leading to the formation of language-based clusters wherein employees seek [emotional support](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/emotional-support "Learn more about emotional support from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from those who share their language ([Ahmad & Barner-Rasmussen, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0004); [Mäkelä et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0051)). Language-based clusters can exacerbate power imbalances as proficient speakers of the corporate language can become gatekeepers by controlling the flow of key information from and to cluster members ([Marschan-Piekkari et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0052); [Neeley & Dumas, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109095162400021X#bib0055)). **INTRODUCTION:**

A language is a part of a culture and a culture is a
part of a language; the two are intricately interwoven so that one cannot
separate the two without losing the significance of either language or
culture. In a word, culture and language are inseparable. I will start by
observing that 20th century anthropological thought that has emphasised the
bijective relationship between language and culture. On the one hand culture
affects and defines linguistic uses, this being the area covered by the
discipline generally known as socio-linguistics. On the other hand it is
language that influences and shapes culture itself, this idea being associated
with the label of linguistic relativism. Linguistic diversity and cultural
identity in the world today is an issue of growing social importance because a
majority of all living languages and cultures are threatened in their continued
existence. How they can be sustained is a matter of study and debate. Changes
in the vitality of a language and culture have important implications for
individuals and societies. Multilingualism is a common and increasing
phenomenon in present day society which can be studied from different perspectives.
Language is used to express and sustain culture and cultural associations that
exist in a given society. Different ideas result from the use of different
languages within a culture.

 

As with biological species, languages and cultures
naturally evolve and change over time. But just as with species, the world is
now undergoing a massive human-made extinction crisis of languages and
cultures. External forces are dispossessing traditional peoples of their lands,
resources, and lifestyles; forcing them to subsist in highly degraded
environments; crushing their cultural traditions or ability to maintain them;
or coercing them into linguistic assimilation and abandonment of ancestral
languages. People who lose their linguistic and cultural identity may lose an
essential element in a social process that commonly teaches respect for nature
and understanding of the natural environment and its processes. Forcing this
cultural and linguistic conversion on indigenous and other traditional peoples
not only violates their human rights, but also undermines the health of the
world's ecosystems and the goals of nature conservation.

 

Culture refers to the customs, practices,
languages, values and world views that define social groups such as those based
on nationality, ethnicity, region or common interests. Cultural identity is
important for peoples sense of self and how they relate to others. A strong
cultural identity can contribute to peoples overall wellbeing. Identifying
with a particular culture helps people feel they belong and gives them a sense
of security. Culturalis a feature of most nations todayas people from different groups live together as a consequence of
historical events and human migrations. Within multilingual societies, the
maintenance of the languages of the various ethnic and cultural groups is
critical for the preservation of cultural heritage and identity. The loss of
language means the loss of culture and identity. In many societies throughout
history, the suppression of the languages of minority groups has been used as a
deliberate policy in order to suppress those minority cultures. As a result a
large number of the world's languages have been lost with the processes of
colonisation and migration. As languages disappear, cultures die. The world
becomes inherently a less interesting place, but we also sacrifice raw
knowledge and the intellectual achievements of millennia.

 

As we can see, we have the perfect
global prerequisites for ruining our planet beyond repair. 1: Long-established
small societies are breaking up, and, with urbanization and migration, people
encounter new environments.2. We do not have the new planets to move to when we
have damaged this one. 3. New technologies are more destructive than ever and
results of biochemical and other experiments are taken into use before we know
anything about the long-term effects on nature or people. 4: We have growing
gaps and alienated elites.
Researchers have a responsibility not only to produce solid knowledge about the
incredible complexities in this transdisciplinary area, but also to act on the
basis of the still (probably always?) incomplete knowledge. It has to be today.

 

Language and culture are intricately related and
dependent on each other. Language is formed by culture, while culture is
influenced and impacted by language. Language is the medium of culture.
 This is clearly seen in immigrant societies. These immigrants are
accustomed to a certain language, and therefore, despite the assimilation, will
continue to use it and keep it alive. To some people, language is identity.
They feel that language is so important to cultural identity that peoples,
governments, and organizations around the world use many different strategies
to affirm their language in a globalizing world. An important anthropological
tradition of thought holds that this relationship is a bijective one. While on
the one hand culture shapes languages, on the other hand it is also formed by
them. That is to say those linguistic differences are the basis of important
peculiarities of cultures and the world visions that support them. This view,
usually called linguistic relativism, completely turns its back on both
semantic realism and cognitive universalism, in order to argue: a) that
linguistic uses determine perceptive and cognitive structures and not vice
versa, and b) that such linguistic uses and relative semantic systems are
basically incommensurable. A fundamental difference in the identification of
meanings on the cross-cultural level follows from this: the inex­tricable
relationship of language, thought and culture (J. Gumperz , S. Levinson, 1996.)
suggests considering each language as being associated with a specific distinct
world vision.

 

Societal life in the 21st century is global, innovative
and adventurous. The infusion of people from countries all over the world
forces societal members to examine the dimensions of ethnicity. Culture and
language are intertwined. Cultural diversity, teaches people to empathize with
and be more sensitive to the needs of others; it helps society recognize and
become more accepting of persons from diverse backgrounds. Linguistic diversity
is the ability to function in one or more languages other than one's own native
language. The ability to connect and interact with others from different
cultures enhances our society by enabling people to share knowledge and gain
greater appreciation of other ethnicities. Culture is transmitted and
reproduced through communication, i.e. through language. As a logical
consequence, a language is the privileged medium of a culture. Language and
Culture Diversity starts from the premise that language diversity is the core
component of cultural diversity, as it enables the transmission of fundamental
aspects of different cultures to succeeding generations and the interaction of
communities belonging to different cultural backgrounds. Language and culture
are those elements that have very much reflected the values and particularities
of different societies, since they are part of the human intangible heritage.

 

Cultural and linguistic diversity, while stimulating
respect for cultural identity, traditions and religions, is essential to the
development of an Information Society based on the dialogue among cultures and
regional and international cooperation. Understanding and valuing cultural
diversity are the keys to countering racism. All individuals must feel free to
explore the uniqueness of their culture and identity while developing
understandings of the cultural diversity that exists in the world around them.
Denying cultural expression means limiting the expression of unique
perspectives on life and the transmission of knowledge from generation to
generation. Whatever, accepting the cultural variability of the language, the
problem that anthropology raises regards the degree of this variability,
whether language depends partly on the context of a specific culture or whether
on the other hand it is also linked to universal type cognitive structures that
do not vary according to the context. How far, then, are linguistic differences
simply variations of a universal meta-language? And, on the other hand, how far
does it concern differences that are somewhat incommensurable? This problem has
been extremely thoroughly examined in the field of cross-cultural semantics.
What happens when we have to translate a language that is deeply rooted in a
culture that is anthropologically very different from our own?

**Language and Cultural Identity:**

Language and identity are inseparably associated with
each other. While language is the medium used by individuals to negotiate a
sense of self in different contexts (Pierce, 1995; Norton, 2000), identity
construction is a social and cultural process which is accomplished through
discursive practices. Therefore, the ability to use a specific language in a
specific context influences the development of cultural identity (Trechter and
Bucholtz, 2001) by creating a tension between the discourse of the dominant
culture and the discourse of the subculture of second language speakers. In
other words, the manner in which language, in this case English, is used
determines to which social group individuals are allowed entrance. Those who
speak English will be admitted to social groups with greater amounts of social
and political power than those who do not (Fairclough, 2001). When the English
language learners (ELLs) first language is devalued and the language of the
socially and politically dominant society is imposed on all of the students in
schools, the ELLs identity is threatened, and inequitable social relationships
between ELLs and native speakers of the dominant language are produced (Pierce,
1995).

 

Ignoring or omitting the cultural aspects of the
language  has been a frequently debated topic in the world of education. According
to the results of many researches, it is found out that the issue of failing to
expose the students to the cultures of the languages that they are learning
makes foreign language education more difficult and less interesting for the
students. Besides, these researches prove that including cultural aspects into
the foreign language curriculum acknowledges students about the other cultures
and enables them to develop empathy towards foreigners. Furthermore, it is also
proven that enhancing the language curriculum with the cultural aspects makes
language learning more sense and much easier for the learners, especially for
the primary school students and for those who have never taken any foreign
language courses before. For a long time, educators in many countries have
understood the importance of teaching cultural information to the foreign
language students and therefore, they included in these information in their
curriculum.

 

It is widely believed that there is a natural
connection between the language spoken by members of a social group and that
groups identity. Throughout history, many cultures and races have been
destroyed or forced to change by other cultures and races. Until recently,
however, many other countries and races have been able to maintain their
cultural and racial identities, more or less protected by borders. As Cummins
(1996) notes, the unequal relation of power between dominant and minority
languages can serve to constrain multiple identities that minority language
speakers can negotiate at school and in society. Moreover, because their
culture is devalued and their language is not supported through primary
language instruction, many second-language-speakers who attend American schools
experience the pressure of a predominant English only ideology and lose their
language and culture altogether (Norton, 2000). The specific measures to
promote the use of regional or minority languages in public life cover the
areas of education, justice, administrative authorities and public services, media,
cultural activities and facilities, economic and social spheres, and
transfrontier exchanges. In sum, the imposition of the predominant English-Only
ideology and the devaluation of the ELLs language and culture force second
language speakers to lose their heritage language and native culture. As a
consequence, they fail to develop both a strong sense of identity and the
cognitive basis for future learning (Sánchez, 1999). Bucholtz and Hall (2004)
characterize language as the most flexible and pervasive symbolic resource
(p. 369) that is at the center of the cultural reproduction of identity. The
cultural identity associated with the politically, economically, and socially
dominant Western European-American culture is seen as the norm and that to which
other social groups in the United States should aspire (Martinez, 2006).

 

 

Although it appears innocuous, the dominant Western
European- American group possesses pervasive and covert power. For example, the
English-Only ideology is seen as a norm in all public schools and institutions.
Students are viewed as having adequate knowledge only if they know English. It
is also understood that English is the language which should be used in all
instructional situations. Moreover, in this time of accountability, the results
of mandated, standardized assessments are only valued if they are administered
in English. These practices not only send the message that other languages and
cultures are not valued in schools, but they also limit English language
learners opportunities for school success (Crawford, 2008).

 

From a critical theory perspective, Watson-Gegeo, and
Gegeo (1999) explain that language is central to cultural ways of thinking.
They argue that language is essential to identity, authenticity, cultural survival
and peoples learning and thinking processes (p.25). This concept is
manifested when English language learners in most American schools must
recreate knowledge through a second language, thereby losing their personal
cultural identity and their authentication of self.  When the link between
language, cultural identity, and ways of thinking limit access to knowledge,
second-language-learners opportunities for literacy development are
diminished.

 

Language is intrinsically related to culture and the
world is becoming more and more intricately multidimensional, both culturally
and linguistically. Language is so deeply embedded in culture that cultural
identity is defined to a great extent in terms of language. Based on this
premise, the loss of one's language contributes to the loss of one's culture.
Local languages are the repositories of traditional knowledge, yet they are
vanishing fast under the pressure of global forces that are also threatening
biological and cultural diversity. For the sake of continuity of the diversity
of life on earth, we must recognize the role of language in the creation,
transmission and perpetuation of local knowledge and cultural behaviors, and
accord indigenous and minority languages the same protection and chances for survival
as are beginning to be granted to the traditional cultures they sustain.
Whatever, these anthropological observations support the linguistic principle
of indexicality, according to which the meaning of words or expressions is
always determined by the specific, concrete context of the social transactions
in which the linguistic practices take place.

 

Linguistic diversity is closely related to ecological
and cultural diversity. The concept of ecosystem is guided by the principle
that living entities exist through a network of interrelationships. The domains
of biological, linguistic and cultural diversities hold a mutually reinforcing
relationship. Human success in inhabiting the earth has been due to human
ability to develop diverse cultures and languages which suit all kinds of
environments. Now it may be argued that if diversity is a prerequisite to
successful humanity then the preservation of linguistic diversity is crucial to
humanity. Crystal (2000: 34) argues that if the development of multiple cultures
is so important then the role of languages becomes critical, for cultures are
chiefly transmitted through spoken and written languages. In the powerfully
written Vanishing Voices, Daniel Nettle and Suzanne Romaine make an explicit
link between language survival and environment issues: the extinction of
languages is part of the larger picture of near-total collapse of the worldwide
ecosystem (as cited in Yadava and Turin (2007)).

 

Cultural revitalization is one way for a nation or a
people to keep their cultural identity from being absorbed into the
industrial-strength blender of globalization. The concept of cultural
identity, intimately connected with those of cultural diversity, and
multiculturalism, has been progressively recognized within the vast category of
human rights and more specifically, of cultural rights, in the scientific
environment and in the practical ones, as well as in the common language. This
could happen also in reaction to the phenomenon of globlisation- not only in
economic sense  which on the contrary, aims to build a dominant culture and a
consequent homologation. This fact threatens the maintenance of the plurality
of cultures.

 

Could increasing of cultural understanding be the most
needed and effective tool for better tolerance and support of language
diversity and minorities? Are not the decrease of biodiversity and the
oppression of a small human being coming from the same root? If we knew
ourselves better and had more holistic and cross-cultural approach to the reality,
then maybe we were better equipped for both sustainable development and global
peace. That cultural diversity has become a political challenge throughout the
world stems from a complex set of factors. One of the major factors of cultural
diversification in various societies is globalization. The intensified flow of
capital, post-Fordist modes of production and the global spread of Western
consumer culture have prompted a variety of social movements that emphasize
their own ethnic, linguistic or religious distinctiveness. The emergence of
transnational migrant networks, facilitated by growing inequalities in the
capitalist world-system as well as by new technologies of transport and
electronic communication, is another prominent aspect of such cultural diversification.
What all these new social movements have in common, whether based on ethnicity,
language or religion, is that they demand full and equal inclusion in society,
while claiming the recognition of their particularistic identities in the public
sphere. They criticize the assumption of congruence between political unity and
cultural homogeneity which was characteristic of the classic model of the
nation-state, and thereby contribute to its far-reaching institutional
transformation.

 

Cultural and linguistic diversity is a feature of most nations today as people from
different groups live together as a consequence of historical events and human
migrations. Within multilingual societies, the maintenance of the languages of
the various ethnic and cultural groups is critical for the preservation of
cultural heritage and identity. The loss of language means the loss of culture
and identity. In many societies throughout history, the suppression of the
languages of minority groups has been used as a deliberate policy in order to
suppress those minority cultures. As a result a large number of the world's
languages have been lost with the processes of colonisation and migration. It
has been estimated that approximately 10,000 spoken languages have existed. Today,
only about 6,000 languages are still spoken and many of these are not being
taught to children. More than half of these languages are unlikely to survive
the next century (W. Davis, 'Vanishing Cultures', in National Geographic, vol.
196, no. 2, pp. 62-89, 1999)

 

India, the largest democracy, is arguably one of the
most culturally diverse nations. Although traditionally nations have shared a
common ethnicity, religion, language and history, India is a multiethnic,
multi-religious, multicultural and multilingual nation of more than one billion
people with a history of assimilating various cultural practices from
conquering civilizations. . At least 800 different languages
and 2000 dialects have been identified in India. The Central government of
India uses Hindi and English languages as the official language of
communication. India has 23 official languages which are used in different
states. Among them, Hindi has been conferred the status of "National
Language". India also has a long heritage of some classical languages like
Pali and Sanskrit.

 

Since her independence, just over sixty four years ago,
India has had to cope with the internal politics of diversity most particularly
concerning language status and use. Thus India is a useful microcosm to analyze
the politics of cultural diversity and the ability of states to have an
influence on internal and external pressures that impact diversity. Despite
Indias attempt to legislate linguistic diversity domestically and promote a
national identity through the promotion of one particular language, the
decreasing diversity of Indian culture as measured through the survival and
utility of her languages serves as a warning for the success of the Convention
on Cultural  Diversity.  For several millennia India has been a multilingual,
multicultural and multiethnic country. With a population of more than 1
billion, comprising around 250 languages (2001 census) spread over 28 states
and 7 union territories; India occupies a distinctly unique position in the
cultural, linguistic and ethnic landscape in the world. There is no single
state in the country which is completely monolingual, not a single major modern
Indian language whose speakers do not employ more than one code and not a
single speech-community which has less than at least three distinct linguistic
codes in its verbal repertoire. The 22 regional languages recognized by the
Indian Constitution enjoy the patronage of the State and lawmakers at the cost
of the rest of languages and their speakers.

 

Further adding complexity to the Indian linguistic
scenario is the recent recommendations of the National Knowledge Commission
(NKC) Report 2006 which stress the English language as both a compulsory
language and as a medium of instruction. The historical legacy repeats itself
in a new avatar which reinforces the appropriateness of English in India. In
this context, not only do the issues of linguistic and cultural diversity and
the situation of Indian languages and especially minority languages need to
be addressed, but the question of identity and identity formation also needs to
be problematized. For the discourse of identity formation located in a
particular space is indicative of ideology which is exclusionary and forms a
cultural practice which is both restrictive and productive.

**Theoretical Approaches to Linguistic Diversity:**

From the earliest philosophers to modern
neuroscientists, researchers from a wide range of disciplines have explored a
diverse range of issues concerning the human capacity for language and the
diversity of the world's languages. Linguists work at the intersection of these
issues and define linguistics as the science of language and languages. During
the last 150 years, linguists have developed a variety of theoretical paradigms
to describe and explain language history, dialect variation, cross-cultural
similarities and differences, the neurological processing and production of
language, and the evolutionary emergence of language. The many different
theoretical approaches to linguistics two major ones of which are: (1) formal
linguistics and (2) functional linguistics have in common that they try to give
linguistic phenomena a theoretical foundation, in particular those phenomena
that vary between languages. Such theoretical research would be rather
uninteresting if it lacked solid empirical foundation, based on a series of
different languages (Bach 1997, 2002, Bakker et al. 1997, Becker-Christiansen and
Widell 2003, Bohn 2004a, Bærentzen 1992, McGregor 2002, Rijkhoff 2003, 2004,
Vikner 2005). Such empirical investigations, i.e. research in linguistic
typology, make up the back bone of comparative linguistics, as they attempt to
establish which types of languages exist and why some are more widespread than
others.

 

In formal Linguistics the explicit theoretical
approaches may be grouped into formal and functional linguistics. In formal
linguistics, it is assumed that only part of the linguistic knowledge of human
beings is acquired and that another part of this knowledge is innate. In other
words, certain aspects of language are the way they are because the human brain
is the way it is. Therefore such theories also have an interesting explanation
to offer as to why certain characteristics do not vary between languages:
Universal features and lack of variation may be derived from the innate part of
the linguistic knowledge of human beings (Vikner 2001, 2004).

 

And functional linguistics attaches crucial importance
to the communicative function of language. The function of linguistic entities
is to communicate a content (which does not necessarily exclude that they also
have non-communicative functions). The fundamental idea is therefore that
language research must be carried out in the light of what we know about how
consciousness handles other kinds of impressions from the outside world and
that consideration for the possibilities of others to understand what is said
plays a crucial role for linguistic form. In other words, functional
linguistics is concerned with the connection between linguistic and
non-linguistic cognitive processes and with the communicative function of
language (Jørgensen 2000, McGregor 1997, Rijkhoff 2002, Togeby 2003).

 

Our linguistic scientific language enterprise depends
on the multiplicity of languages and the knowledge of linguistic diversity.
Similarly, our understanding of linguistic typology and our ability to classify
languages accurately and reconstruct proto-form depends on the availability of
a wide array of languages. There was a time when linguistics was inextricably
tied up with the study of non-written, non-western languages, but this is not
